title: Database — Server Health
description: Top-level server-health signals: liveness, saturation, throughput, efficiency, durability subsystem, capacity. Per-query / per-table / per-backend-type (pg_stat_io) forensics live in the 'Database — Query & Table Performance' dashboard — this one is intentionally high-level so it stays readable during an incident. Scope of the `Exporter job` dropdown: gates the two liveness panels only (Postgres up stat, and Scrape liveness history). Data panels (connection utilization, TPS, cache hit, WAL, checkpoints, storage) always render against their full upstream series regardless of selection — they don't depend on which exporter's `up` signal you're inspecting.

panels.title: Postgres up
panels.description: Composite liveness: `min(up over selected exporters) × (max(pg_up) or vector(0))`. Flips to DOWN (red) when any selected exporter scrape fails OR when postgres-exporter scrapes successfully but reports pg_up=0 (i.e. it cannot reach Postgres). Renders UNKNOWN (yellow) when BOTH arms are absent — neither the selected exporter nor postgres-exporter are producing samples — because that's an instrumentation failure, not a DB failure, and on-call should diagnose it differently. Uses vector multiplication rather than `scalar()` so the expression is stable if a second postgres-exporter target is ever added (scalar() would return NaN on multi-series input and the tile would silently go blank). The `$job` dropdown only gates this panel and the Scrape liveness history panel directly below it; all other panels render their full data regardless of selection.

panels.title: Connection utilization
panels.description: Connected backends (to wealthpay + any user DB; template0/template1/postgres excluded) / `max_connections`. `numbackends` counts ALL connected sessions including idle ones — this is deliberate, because an idle session still consumes a connection slot, and slot exhaustion is the #1 DB outage cause. Sustained >80% is the canonical warning threshold. Expression avoids `scalar()` on the divisor: `max(pg_settings_max_connections)` is used instead so the panel degrades to no-data rather than NaN if postgres-exporter reports multiple PG instances in the future. `clamp_min(..., 1)` guards against divide-by-zero; max_connections is never 0 on a live PG but the guard is essentially free and closes a footgun.

panels.title: Replication slot lag (Debezium)
panels.description: `pg_replication_slots_pg_wal_lsn_diff` in bytes, filtered to `slot_name=~"debezium.*"`. Sustained growth = Debezium is not keeping up; WAL cannot be reclaimed until the consumer drains. If this climbs unboundedly, the DB disk will fill. Explicit slot-name filter (rather than `max by (slot_name)`) so that adding a second replication slot later — e.g. a physical replica — does not silently change which slot this tile reports on. Current thresholds (100 MiB warn / 1 GiB crit) sized against ~1 MB/s WAL production at baseline: warn ≈ 100 s of lag, crit ≈ 17 min — both recoverable before disk pressure becomes acute, both well above normal end-to-end Debezium latency jitter.

panels.title: Rollback ratio (5m)
panels.description: Rollbacks / (commits + rollbacks) over 5m — the DB-side view of how many recent transactions ended in ROLLBACK rather than COMMIT. Only computed when the combined transaction rate exceeds 0.5 tps; below that threshold the panel gaps deliberately (a handful of rollbacks on an otherwise quiet DB would make the ratio jump to ~1.0 and flash red for no operational reason — postgres-exporter's own probe traffic and stray `ROLLBACK` from monitoring sessions are enough to swamp a truly idle denominator). Mirrors the `and rate(...) > 0` gating pattern already used on the WAL latency panels. Important context for this event-sourced stack: a ROLLBACK here is not necessarily an application error — optimistic-concurrency conflicts on `event_store(aggregate_id, version)` are a designed retry path, and under Gatling load they can dominate the ratio. Cross-reference with the app-side `wealthpay_account_command_*` timer's `outcome="error"` tag before concluding the DB is reflecting a user-visible incident.

panels.title: Transactions per second
panels.description: Commits and rollbacks per second for the wealthpay database. Load shape — a flat line when app traffic is known to be high is a red flag (connection pool exhausted, or scrape broken). Y-axis uses `symlog` scaling (linear below 1, log above) so that a single load-test spike at hundreds of tps does not visually crush the 0-5 tps baseline, which is usually the diagnostic signal. Visual caveat: on symlog, a line hugging the x-axis can be either 0 tps or <0.01 tps — they look identical. If you need to distinguish true-idle from near-idle (e.g. during a traffic-drop incident), hover the line for the exact value, or cross-check against the block hits/reads panel below.

panels.title: Buffer cache hit ratio
panels.description: `blks_hit / (blks_hit + blks_read)` over 5m. When this falls off a cliff, the working set has exceeded shared_buffers and PG is going to disk. Expected steady-state is >0.99 for an OLTP workload. Idle periods (zero blocks read or hit) render as a GAP, not a flat line — that is deliberate: a flat-100% panel during idle would be visually indistinguishable from a healthy high-traffic panel, masking whether the DB is actually being exercised. Correlate with the Transactions per second panel above to read this one: if TPS is non-zero and this is flat, the cache is healthy; if this is a gap, there's no traffic to judge.

panels.title: Block hits / reads
panels.description: Raw rates behind the hit ratio — when the ratio dips, look here to see whether hits dropped or reads spiked (the former is concerning, the latter is usually a large scan).

panels.title: WAL production rate
panels.description: `rate(pg_wal_stat_bytes_total[5m])` — bytes of WAL generated per second. The original 'excessive WAL' signal from the Step 3 brief.

panels.title: WAL backpressure (buffers_full rate)
panels.description: Sustained non-zero rate means `wal_buffers` is undersized — the server is stalling on WAL buffer flushes. This is a tuning signal, not a transient alert.

panels.title: WAL fsync latency (avg ms / sync)
panels.description: `rate(sync_time_ms) / rate(sync_count)` — average milliseconds per WAL fsync over 5 minutes. Requires `track_wal_io_timing=on` (see docker-compose.local.yml). This is the earliest signal of a WAL device stall: rising fsync time means commits are queuing behind disk flushes. Will be wired to a Prometheus alert in the 'Calibrated alert rules' milestone. Averaging caveat: this is a mean across the 5-minute window, so a single short fsync stall (e.g. one 500 ms hiccup amid 300 normal fsyncs) is smoothed away — by design, counters can't surface p99 without a histogram. Downstream alerts on this signal must be written for SUSTAINED elevation (e.g. `> threshold for 5m`), not spikes. Short-stall detection is a separate workstream that would require `pg_stat_statements`-driven slow-commit counts or a histogram collector.

panels.title: WAL write latency (avg ms / write)
panels.description: `rate(write_time_ms) / rate(write_count)` — average milliseconds per WAL write over 5 minutes. Pair with the fsync latency panel: a spike in write latency without fsync latency points at WAL-buffer contention; a spike in both points at the underlying device. Same averaging caveat as the fsync panel — short stalls inside the 5m window are smoothed away, and downstream alerts must target sustained elevation, not spikes.

panels.title: Checkpoints per 5 min (timed vs requested)
panels.description: Count of checkpoints that happened in each rolling 5-minute window. `increase([5m])` rather than `rate([5m])` on purpose: checkpoints are discrete, low-cadence events (~1 per 5 min timed, rare for requested), so derivative-based rates either alias into sawtooth (short window) or suppress rare events into invisibility (long window). The honest abstraction is `count per window` — a human reads '2 timed, 1 requested' and immediately knows what happened. Any non-zero requested value is a tuning signal: WAL filled before the checkpoint timer, so bump `max_wal_size` or shorten `checkpoint_timeout`. The requested series is rendered in red specifically so a single tick during a load burst draws the eye. PG17 upgrade hazard: these columns move from pg_stat_bgwriter to the new pg_stat_checkpointer view on PG17 — postgres-exporter gates them behind the opt-in `--collector.stat_checkpointer` flag (default off), so these series will go silently flat on upgrade until that flag is set. See docs/postgres-upgrade.md.

panels.title: Checkpoint busy fraction (write + sync phases)
panels.description: Fraction of wall-clock time the checkpointer spent in its write or sync phase over each 5-minute window. 0.9 means the checkpointer was busy 90% of that window — which is the default steady-state for large checkpoints (checkpoint_completion_target=0.9). Treat sustained values >0.95 as tuning pressure, not a per-checkpoint duration. Computed as `increase(ms_counter[5m]) / 300000` — milliseconds-of-phase divided by milliseconds-of-window. The 5-minute window is deliberately aligned with the adjacent checkpoints panel so the same tick appears on both charts under the same temporal lens. Same PG17 hazard: columns relocate to pg_stat_checkpointer on PG17 and require postgres-exporter's opt-in `--collector.stat_checkpointer` collector. See docs/postgres-upgrade.md.

panels.title: Storage footprint
panels.description: Database size (content) and WAL directory size (liveness). If WAL size diverges upward from DB size, replication lag or archiver failure is usually the cause — cross-reference with the Replication slot lag tile at the top. Scope caveat: DB size is scoped to `datname=wealthpay` while WAL is server-wide — the visual comparison only holds on a single-DB stack. On a multi-DB server, sum `pg_database_size_bytes` across the databases you care about before comparing. Y-axis minimum is pinned to 0 on purpose: an auto-scaled bytes axis makes a WAL runaway look visually "close to the DB line" because both lines shift in concert, masking the divergence.

panels.title: Scrape liveness (history)
panels.description: Per-exporter `up` over the selected time range. Complements the top-left `Postgres up` stat, which shows only the current moment. Two distinct failure shapes to read here: (1) a step down to 0 means the exporter was scraped but reported itself DOWN — a real connectivity or auth problem inside the exporter; (2) a gap in the line means no sample was recorded at all, which on a local dev stack most often means Prometheus itself wasn't scraping (laptop sleep, docker paused, Prometheus restarted). The two are operationally very different — a 0-step pages someone, a gap does not. `connectNulls=false` is intentional so gaps render as gaps.
